teaching.title: The Java Program Skeleton
teaching.analogy: Every Java program is like a building — it MUST have a foundation (class), a main entrance (main method), and each floor ends with a semicolon (;). Miss any part and the building collapses!
teaching.content: Java requires a specific structure for every program. You need: a class, a main method, and statements ending with semicolons. Unlike Python, Java won't run without this skeleton.

examples.title: Minimal Java Program
examples.explanation: This is the smallest valid Java program. Every piece is required.

examples.title: Python vs Java
examples.explanation: See how much more structure Java requires!

teaching.funFact: Java was created in 1995 by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems. Its motto was 'Write Once, Run Anywhere'! ☕

teaching.title: System.out.println()
teaching.analogy: System.out.println() is Java's loudspeaker. System = the computer, out = the output screen, println = print line. It's verbose but crystal clear about what it does!
teaching.content: System.out.println() displays text on screen and moves to a new line. System.out.print() stays on the same line. Text must be in double quotes.

examples.title: println vs print
examples.explanation: println adds a newline, print does not.
examples.code:
System.out.println("Line 1"); // Line 1↵
System.out.println("Line 2"); // Line 2↵
System.out.print("No");
System.out.print("Break");   // NoBreak

examples.title: Printing Numbers
examples.explanation: Numbers don't need quotes in Java.
examples.code:
System.out.println(42);      // 42
System.out.println(3.14);    // 3.14
System.out.println(10 + 5);  // 15

teaching.funFact: Java uses double quotes for Strings. Single quotes are only for single characters (char type)! 🔤

teaching.title: Semicolons & Curly Braces
teaching.analogy: Semicolons are like periods ending sentences. Curly braces {} are like chapter boundaries that group code belonging together.
teaching.content: Every statement in Java ends with a semicolon ;. Curly braces {} define blocks — the class body, method body, if-blocks, loops. Forgetting either causes a compile error.

examples.title: Where ; and {} go
examples.explanation: Statements end with ;. Blocks are wrapped in {}.

teaching.funFact: If even ONE semicolon is missing, the entire Java program refuses to compile! 🚨

mcq.q: What is the correct method signature to start a Java program?
mcq.explanation: Entry Point: public static void main(String[] args) is Java's required entry point. The JVM looks for this exact signature to know where to start execution.

mcq.q: Which statement correctly prints 'Hello' in Java?
mcq.explanation: Output Channel: System.out.println() is Java's standard output method. System.out is the output stream, println() writes text followed by a newline character.

mcq.q: What does every Java statement end with?
mcq.explanation: Syntax Rule: Every Java statement must end with ;. This tells the compiler where one instruction ends and the next begins.

mcq.q: What do curly braces {} define in Java?
options: Blocks of code that belong together
mcq.explanation: Block Intel: Curly braces {} define a code block — the body of a class, method, if-statement, or loop.

mcq.q: In Java, String values must be wrapped in ___.
mcq.explanation: String Syntax: Java uses double quotes for String values. Single quotes are reserved for char (single character) only.

coding.title: Complete the Output
coding.objective: What Java method prints text and moves to new line? Fill in the blank.

coding.title: Spot the Ending
coding.objective: Every Java statement must end with what character?
coding.hint: It looks like a period with a comma below it.

coding.title: Identify the Error
coding.hint: Every Java statement needs this terminator.

options: Missing semicolon at the end of the println statement
Double quotes should be single quotes
debugging.answer: Missing semicolon at the end of the println statement
debugging.hint: Every Java statement must end with ;

options: 'system' must be capitalized to 'System' — Java is case sensitive
debugging.answer: 'system' must be capitalized to 'System' — Java is case sensitive
debugging.hint: System (capital S) is the correct class name. Java is case sensitive.

options: Missing opening curly brace { after class Main
debugging.answer: Missing opening curly brace { after class Main
debugging.hint: Every class declaration must be followed by { to open the code block.

prediction.explanation: Each println adds a newline. Three separate print calls = three separate lines of output.
print() without 'ln' does NOT add a newline — both outputs appear on the same line.

teaching.title: Variables in Java — Declare the Type First!
teaching.analogy: In Java, variables are labeled jars where you must say what KIND of jar it is (int, String, double) BEFORE putting anything inside. Python lets you use any jar — Java makes you commit to a type upfront.
teaching.content: Java requires you to declare the TYPE of a variable before using it. Format: type name = value; — e.g., int score = 100;. The type cannot change after declaration.

examples.title: Java Variable Declarations
examples.explanation: Type always comes first.

examples.title: Python vs Java
examples.explanation: Java is explicit, Python is implicit.

teaching.funFact: Java is 'statically typed' — types checked at compile time. Python is 'dynamically typed' — types checked at runtime. Static typing catches bugs early! 🔍

teaching.title: camelCase & Reassignment
teaching.analogy: Java naming convention is camelCase — like a camel's humps. First word lowercase, each next word capitalized: playerScore, firstName, isGameOver.
teaching.content: Variable naming rules: start with letter or _, no spaces, case sensitive. Convention: use camelCase. You can reassign without re-stating the type. You CANNOT change the type once declared.

examples.title: camelCase in action
examples.explanation: Java uses camelCase — not snake_case like Python.

examples.title: Cannot Change Type
examples.explanation: Once int, always int.

teaching.funFact: Java class names use PascalCase (MyClass). Variables/methods use camelCase (myVariable). The compiler doesn't enforce style, but all Java devs follow this convention! 📐

mcq.q: Which is the correct Java variable declaration?
mcq.explanation: Declaration Syntax: Java requires: type name = value; — int is the type, score is the name, 100 is the value, ; ends the statement.

mcq.q: After 'int x = 5;', can you write 'x = "hello";'?
options: No — x is int and cannot store a String
mcq.explanation: Type Lock: Once declared as int, x can ONLY hold integers. This is Java's static typing — it catches type mismatches at compile time.

mcq.q: Which follows Java's camelCase convention?
mcq.explanation: Naming Convention: Java variables use camelCase — first word lowercase, subsequent words start with uppercase: playerScore, isGameOver.

mcq.q: When reassigning a variable, do you repeat the type keyword?
mcq.explanation: Reassignment Rule: The type keyword is only used during declaration. To update the value, just write: variableName = newValue;

mcq.q: What does 'String name = "Alex";' create?
mcq.explanation: Variable Intel: This declares a variable 'name' of type String and stores the text 'Alex' in it.

coding.title: Declare an Integer
coding.objective: What type keyword is used for whole numbers in Java?
coding.hint: int = integer — it holds whole numbers like 5, 100, -42.

coding.title: String Declaration
coding.objective: What Java type holds text? Fill in to store 'Neo'.
coding.hint: Java's text type starts with uppercase S — it's a class.

coding.title: Reassign Without Type
coding.objective: After 'int score = 0;', what's the correct way to change score to 100?
coding.hint: Just use the variable name — no type keyword needed for reassignment.

options: Missing type declaration — should be: int x = 42;
debugging.answer: Missing type declaration — should be: int x = 42;
debugging.hint: Java requires a type when first declaring a variable.
Java is case sensitive. score and Score are completely different identifiers.

prediction.explanation: Both x and y are int. x + y = 10 + 3 = 13. Integer addition.
100 - 30 = 70, then 70 - 20 = 50. Each line reassigns hp with the new result.

teaching.title: Java's Primitive Types
teaching.analogy: Java's primitives are specific-purpose containers: int for whole numbers, double for decimals, boolean for ON/OFF, char for exactly ONE character.
teaching.content: Java has 8 primitive types. The 4 you'll use most: int (whole numbers), double (decimals), boolean (true/false), char (single character in single quotes).

examples.title: The 4 Core Primitives
examples.explanation: Each stores a different kind of data.
examples.code:
int lives = 3;           // whole number
double price = 9.99;     // decimal
boolean isWinner = true; // true or false
char grade = 'A';        // ONE char, single quotes

examples.title: Type Limits
examples.explanation: Each type has memory limits.

teaching.funFact: Java's char is 2 bytes (not 1 like in C) because it supports Unicode — characters from every language on Earth! 🌍

teaching.title: String — The Object Type
teaching.analogy: String is like a necklace made of char beads. While primitives are simple raw values, String is a CLASS — a complex object. That's why it starts with capital S!
teaching.content: String is not a primitive — it's a class. It has built-in methods. Use double quotes for String values. Strings can be joined with the + operator.

examples.title: String Methods
examples.explanation: String has methods because it's a class.
examples.code:
String name = "Alex";
System.out.println(name.length());      // 4
System.out.println(name.toUpperCase()); // ALEX
System.out.println(name + " Smith");   // Alex Smith

examples.title: char vs String
examples.explanation: char = single quotes, ONE character. String = double quotes.
examples.code:
char letter = 'A';      // single quotes
String word = "Hello";  // double quotes
String one = "A";       // String, NOT char!

teaching.funFact: Java Strings are IMMUTABLE — methods like toUpperCase() return a NEW String; they don't modify the original! 🔒

mcq.q: Which Java type stores decimal numbers like 9.99?
mcq.explanation: Type Intel: double stores floating-point (decimal) numbers. Use double for prices, measurements, or any value needing decimals.

mcq.q: What is wrong with: char grade = "A";
options: char uses single quotes: char grade = 'A';
mcq.explanation: Char Rule: char uses single quotes — 'A'. Double quotes "A" create a String. char can hold EXACTLY ONE character.

mcq.q: Why does 'String' start with a capital S?
options: String is a class (object type), not a primitive
mcq.explanation: Class Convention: Class names start with uppercase in Java. String is a class, so it's capitalized. int, double, boolean, char are primitives — lowercase.

mcq.q: What does 'boolean isReady = true;' store?
mcq.explanation: Boolean Intel: boolean stores true or false (both lowercase in Java — unlike Python's True/False). It's the foundation of all decisions in your code.

mcq.q: Output of: System.out.println("Hi" + " " + "Java");
mcq.explanation: Concatenation: + joins Strings. "Hi" + " " + "Java" = "Hi Java". The space string " " adds the gap.

coding.title: Decimal Type
coding.objective: What Java type stores decimals like 3.14?

coding.title: Single Character
coding.objective: What type holds exactly ONE character with single quotes?
coding.hint: Single quotes ' ' are used for this primitive type.

coding.title: Boolean Value
coding.objective: What is the correct Java boolean for true — capital or lowercase?
coding.hint: All lowercase in Java — unlike Python's True.

options: char uses single quotes: char letter = 'B';
debugging.answer: char uses single quotes: char letter = 'B';
debugging.hint: char values go in single quotes 'B'. Double quotes make it a String.

options: Java boolean is lowercase: 'true' not 'True' (that's Python)
debugging.answer: Java boolean is lowercase: 'true' not 'True' (that's Python)
debugging.hint: Unlike Python (True/False with capital), Java uses: true / false (all lowercase).

prediction.explanation: When int and double mix, Java promotes the result to double. 5 + 2.0 = 7.0.
length() returns the character count. 'J','a','v','a' = 4 characters.

teaching.title: The Scanner Class
teaching.analogy: Scanner is like a ticket booth operator — it stands at the keyboard and reads whatever the user types, then hands it to your program. You must first set up the booth (create Scanner), then ask it to read.
teaching.content: Java reads input using the Scanner class. You must import it, create an instance connected to System.in (keyboard), then call methods to read different types of input.

examples.title: Scanner Setup & Use
examples.explanation: Import → Create → Read. Three steps always.

examples.title: Reading Different Types
examples.explanation: Use the right method for each type.
examples.code:
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
int age = sc.nextInt();       // reads integer
double price = sc.nextDouble(); // reads decimal
String name = sc.nextLine();  // reads full line of text

teaching.funFact: Python's input() is one function that always returns String. Java's Scanner has separate methods per type: nextInt(), nextDouble(), nextLine() — more explicit but more precise! 🎯

teaching.title: nextLine() vs nextInt()
teaching.analogy: nextInt() is like picking only the first apple from a bag and leaving the bag on the counter. nextLine() grabs everything that's left in the bag. Be careful mixing them — leftover 'Enter' keys cause bugs!
teaching.content: nextInt() reads only the number — it leaves the newline (Enter key) in the buffer. nextLine() reads the whole line including spaces. Mixing them requires an extra sc.nextLine() to clear the buffer.

examples.title: Safe Pattern
examples.explanation: Always close the Scanner when done.

teaching.funFact: Forgetting sc.close() causes a resource leak warning in Java. Always close your Scanner when you're done reading! 🔒

mcq.q: What must you import to use Scanner in Java?
mcq.explanation: Import Rule: Scanner lives in the java.util package. You must write 'import java.util.Scanner;' at the top of your file before using it.

mcq.q: What does 'Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);' do?
options: Creates a Scanner connected to the keyboard
mcq.explanation: Object Creation: This creates a Scanner object 'sc' connected to System.in (the keyboard). Now sc can read whatever the user types.

mcq.q: Which method reads a full line of text from the user?
mcq.explanation: Input Method: sc.nextLine() reads everything the user types until they press Enter, including spaces. sc.next() only reads one word.

mcq.q: What method reads an integer from user input?
mcq.explanation: Type Reading: sc.nextInt() reads and returns the next integer the user types. Use sc.nextDouble() for decimals.

coding.title: Import Scanner
coding.objective: What package does Scanner live in? Complete the import.
coding.hint: The class name is Scanner — same as what you use in code.

coding.title: Read a String
coding.objective: Which Scanner method reads a full line of text?
coding.hint: It reads everything until the user presses Enter.

coding.title: Read an Integer
coding.objective: Which Scanner method reads a whole number?

options: Missing 'import java.util.Scanner;' at the top of the file
debugging.answer: Missing 'import java.util.Scanner;' at the top of the file
debugging.hint: Scanner requires 'import java.util.Scanner;' before the class declaration.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug — wrong method for reading text:

int name = sc.nextInt();
options: To store text, use String name = sc.nextLine(); — not int and nextInt()
debugging.answer: To store text, use String name = sc.nextLine(); — not int and nextInt()
debugging.hint: nextInt() reads integers. For text/names, use nextLine() with String type.

teaching.title: Comments in Java
teaching.analogy: Comments are sticky notes for humans. Java ignores them completely — they're invisible to the compiler but gold for developers reading your code later.
teaching.content: Java supports two comment styles: // for single-line comments and /* ... */ for multi-line comments. Javadoc comments /** ... */ are used to generate documentation.

examples.title: Comment Styles
examples.explanation: Three types of comments in Java.

examples.title: Practical Comments
examples.explanation: Comments explain the WHY, not the WHAT.

teaching.funFact: Javadoc comments /** */ can automatically generate HTML documentation for your code. Major Java libraries like the JDK itself are documented this way! 📚

teaching.title: Java Conventions & Keywords
teaching.analogy: Java has reserved parking spots (keywords) that only the language can use. Convention is the unwritten dress code all Java developers follow — not enforced by the compiler but respected by every team.
teaching.content: Java has 50+ keywords: int, class, public, static, void, if, else, for, while, true, false, return, new, import, etc. Java conventions: PascalCase for classes, camelCase for variables/methods, UPPER_CASE for constants.

examples.title: Naming Conventions Summary
examples.explanation: The convention every Java developer follows.

teaching.funFact: Java has more reserved keywords than Python — 51 vs Python's 35. Keywords like 'goto' and 'const' are reserved but not actually used in Java! They exist to prevent confusion. 🤯

mcq.q: How do you write a single-line comment in Java?
mcq.explanation: Comment Syntax: Java uses // for single-line comments. Python uses #. Java also supports /* */ for multi-line blocks.

mcq.q: Which naming convention does Java use for class names?
mcq.explanation: Class Convention: Java class names use PascalCase — every word starts with uppercase: PlayerScore, BattleEngine, DataManager.

mcq.q: Which is a valid Java keyword?
mcq.explanation: Keyword Intel: 'static' is a Java keyword defining class-level members. You cannot use Java keywords (int, class, public, static, void etc.) as variable names.

mcq.q: How do you declare a constant in Java?
mcq.explanation: Constant Declaration: Java uses the 'final' keyword to declare constants. Once set, a final variable cannot be changed. By convention, constants use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.

coding.title: Single Line Comment
coding.objective: What symbol starts a single-line comment in Java?
coding.hint: Java uses two forward slashes — different from Python's single #

coding.title: Constant Declaration
coding.objective: What keyword makes a variable's value permanently unchangeable in Java?
coding.hint: This keyword locks the value so it can never be reassigned.

coding.title: Class Naming
coding.objective: Which correctly follows Java's class naming convention?
coding.hint: Classes use PascalCase — every word starts with uppercase.

debugging.brokenCode: # This is a comment in Java
options: Java uses // not # for single-line comments
debugging.answer: Java uses // not # for single-line comments
debugging.hint: In Java, single-line comments start with //. Python uses #.

options: 'final' variables cannot be reassigned — maxHp is a constant
Should be int not final int
debugging.answer: 'final' variables cannot be reassigned — maxHp is a constant
debugging.hint: The 'final' keyword makes a variable a constant — it can never be changed after initialization.

prediction.explanation: The first line is a comment (starts with //) — Java ignores it. Only 'Visible' is printed.
final just means the value can't be changed — you can still read and print it normally.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE JVM
teaching.analogy: The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is the ultimate judge. It compiles your code, checks every type, every semicolon, every brace. One mistake and it REJECTS everything. Prove you've mastered Java Level 1!
teaching.content: THE JVM has absorbed all of Level 1's Java knowledge: program structure, System.out.println, typed variables, data types (int/double/boolean/char/String), Scanner input, and Java style conventions. Defeat it to earn your 'First Java Code' badge!

examples.title: What you've mastered
examples.explanation: Everything from Java Level 1!
examples.code:
✅ public class + main() + semicolons
✅ System.out.println() vs print()
✅ int, double, boolean, char, String
✅ Typed variable declarations
✅ Scanner for user input
✅ // comments, final, camelCase

teaching.funFact: The JVM compiles your Java code to bytecode first, then interprets that bytecode. This is why Java is 'compiled AND interpreted' — and why it can 'Write Once, Run Anywhere'! ⚡

mcq.q:
What is wrong with this code?
public class main { }
options: Class name should be capitalized: 'Main' not 'main' (PascalCase)
mcq.explanation: Convention Check: Java class names use PascalCase. 'main' (lowercase) doesn't follow convention and will confuse the JVM looking for the main entry point.
Java evaluates x * x = 4 * 4 = 16 before passing it to println. Output: 16.

options: Line 2 — can't assign String to an int variable
mcq.explanation: Type Error: score was declared as int. Attempting to store a String in it causes a compile-time type mismatch error.

mcq.q: What does 'final' do when applied to a variable?
mcq.explanation: Constant Lock: final makes a variable a constant — its value is set once and can never be changed. Convention: name constants in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.

mcq.q: What is output of: System.out.println("Score: " + 100);
mcq.explanation: Concatenation: When + is used between a String and a number, Java converts the number to String and concatenates. "Score: " + 100 = "Score: 100".

coding.title: Boss Challenge: Type the Declaration
coding.objective: Declare a double variable 'gpa' with value 3.8. What type keyword goes first?
coding.hint: double is Java's type for decimal numbers.

coding.title: Boss Challenge: Complete the Program
coding.objective: What is the entry point method name in a Java program?
coding.hint: It's the one method the JVM looks for to start execution.

debugging.hint: Every Java statement ends with ; — including variable declarations.

prediction.explanation: a + b with two ints = 7. But "" + a + b starts string concatenation: "" + 3 = "3", then "3" + 4 = "34". Order matters!
